_comment: Whisper ASR per-tensor tolerances for compare_tensors.py.
Regime: F32 GGUF + F32 KV cache + production C++ MelFrontend.
validate.py exercises the full mel→encoder→decoder pipeline by
default so any frontend regression (precision, windowing, padding,
filterbank) lights up here instead of slipping past as it did with
the 4613129 mel rewrite. Production variants (F16 model, quants,
F16 KV cache) are validated separately under Stage 5/6/7; their
drift does NOT flow into the numbers below.
Debug knob: `validate.py cpp --mel-from-ref` (and `validate.py all
--mel-from-ref`) sets TRANSCRIBE_WHISPER_MEL_FROM_REF so enc.mel.in
becomes bit-identical to HF's WhisperFeatureExtractor output. Use
this when a per-tensor regression fires and you want to isolate
whether the drift originates in the C++ mel or downstream in the
graph. Numbers below are from the default (C++ mel) regime; running
with --mel-from-ref will tighten enc.mel.in to exactly 0 and
narrow every downstream tensor.
Reference: transformers 5.6.1 (WhisperForConditionalGeneration) on
CPU, fp32 inference. Reference mel is the canonical WhisperFeature-
Extractor output (slaney filterbank + whisper_logmel compression).
Reference decoder is run as a single prefill pass over the prompt
[<|sot|>, <|lang|>, <|transcribe|>, <|notimestamps|>] where <|lang|>
is the per-case language declared in the manifest (jfk → <|en|>
id 50259, german → <|de|> id 50261). validate.py threads this
through to both ref and C++ so both sides see the same prompt.
C++: ggml CPU backend, weights stored as F32 in GGUF (whisper-tiny
ships F32 upstream). Flash-attention is available on both paths but
is a no-op on numerics — encoder drift measured identical with
TRANSCRIBE_NO_FLASH=1, so this file does not name flash as a source.
Decoder runs through the KV-cached path by default: cross K/V are
precomputed once from the encoder output into the cross cache; self
K/V are written to the self cache as each token is processed. With
F32 cache the round-trip is lossless, so drift matches the non-
cached path within reproducible fp32 scheduler noise.
History: prior to the GELU erf-variant fix (switch from ggml_gelu
tanh-approximation to ggml_gelu_erf to match HF's nn.functional.gelu)
observed drift was 300-3000× larger across every tensor — e.g.
enc.final was ~1.5 max_abs, dec.logits_raw was ~0.18. Post-fix the
regime is clean fp32 reduction-order noise; every tensor's max_abs
is now under 0.01% of its peak signal magnitude. Git history carries
the earlier numbers if a regression post-mortem ever needs them.
Tolerance recipe: uniform 1.5× worst-of-{jfk, german} observed drift,
rounded up in the last significant figure. Leaves enough headroom to
absorb fp32 reduction-order noise across machines but tight enough
that any per-tensor 5× regression fails validate.py. Zero-drift
tensors (GGUF reads + pure adds) are pinned at exact 0.0 — any
nonzero drift there indicates an unintended dtype conversion.
Escape hatches (these change the regime — measure separately, do
not conflate with the default numbers below):
- --mel-from-ref                 : ref-mel injection; tightens
enc.mel.in to 0 and narrows
every downstream tensor
- --kv-type f16                  : F16 KV cache (~8× wider max)
Encoder drift profile (values from the worst of {jfk, german}):
Stem (conv stem) — clean.
enc.mel.in      C++ MelFrontend vs HF WhisperFeatureExtractor.
Worst observed drift on jfk + german has been
~3.4e-4 max @ peak 1.4 (rel ~2.5e-4) in the
fp64 frontend regime; the fp32 mixed-radix
rewrite (4613129) shifts this and propagates
into every downstream encoder tensor — re-
measure with the current code before treating
the numbers below as load-bearing. With
--mel-from-ref this tensor pins to exactly 0.
enc.pos_emb     0                 (raw weight read from GGUF)
enc.conv1.out   4.3e-6 max,       (fp32 conv reduction order;
5.7e-8 mean        single matmul + erf GELU)
enc.conv2.out   1.6e-5 max,       (stride-2 conv; slightly worse
2.1e-7 mean        than conv1 due to an extra
reduction axis)
enc.embed.out   matches conv2.out (add of a zero-drift pos_emb)
Transformer blocks — cumulative fp32 matmul drift.
enc.block.0.out 1.4e-5 max,       (~3e-4% of peak signal 4.4)
5.7e-7 mean
enc.block.1.out 2.9e-5 max,       (~3e-4% of peak signal 9.7)
8.6e-7 mean
enc.block.2.out 7.0e-3 max,       (peak signal ~242 at this
1.7e-6 mean        layer; drift is ~0.003%
relative; mean stays tight)
enc.block.3.out 8.1e-3 max,       (peak ~265; ~0.003% relative)
4.6e-6 mean
enc.final       1.5e-3 max,       (post final LN; peak 17.4;
3.7e-6 mean        ~0.009% relative)
Decoder drift profile (values from the worst of {jfk, german}, F32
KV-cached path on whisper-tiny-F32.gguf, prompt pass seq_len=4):
Embedding stage — clean.
dec.token_emb   0                 (ggml_get_rows on the F32 GGUF
embedding table; bit-identical)
dec.pos_emb     0                 (ggml_get_rows on the F32 GGUF
positional embedding table)
dec.embed_sum   0                 (add of two zero-drift tensors)
Transformer blocks — cumulative fp32 matmul drift. The F32 cache
round-trip is lossless, so the cached decoder reproduces a
hypothetical no-cache run within fp32 scheduler noise.
Post-GELU-fix, max_abs on blocks 1/2/3 is ~9e-5 across the board
(german case slightly worse than jfk).
dec.block.0.out 6.7e-6 max,       (~7e-5% of peak signal ~10)
2.6e-7 mean
dec.block.1.out 9.2e-5 max,       (~1e-4% of peak ~81)
5.3e-7 mean
dec.block.2.out 9.2e-5 max,       (~1e-4% of peak ~82)
6.6e-7 mean
dec.block.3.out 9.2e-5 max,       (~1e-4% of peak ~77)
1.3e-6 mean
dec.out_before_head
3.9e-4 max,       (post final LN; peak ~229;
1.9e-5 mean        ~2e-4% relative)
dec.logits_raw  6.9e-5 max,       (vocab projection; peak ~37;
1.9e-5 mean        ~2e-4% relative)
dec.logits      7.5e-5 max,       (log_softmax over vocab)
2.1e-5 mean
Dominant drift mechanism: fp32 matmul reduction-order divergence
between ggml CPU kernels and torch.nn.Linear's BLAS. No layer-norm
eps mismatch (both 1e-5), no activation-variant mismatch (both use
exact erf-based GELU since the 2026-04 fix), no attention-path
divergence (flash on/off identical), no layout bug (first/last
elements match sign and magnitude on every tensor; drift is
distributed, not structural). Typical p99.9 drift per tensor is 1-2
orders of magnitude below the reported max_abs.
Attenuation pattern: mildly GROWING through both encoder and decoder
— expected for compounding fp32 error. Decoder blocks plateau after
block 1 because peak signal plateaus (~77-82); encoder's block 2/3
jump (1e-5 → 7-8e-3) tracks the model's internal signal magnitude
growth to ~242-265 at those layers, not a compute-path regression.
No _suspects: every tensor's max_abs is under 0.01% of peak signal.
enc.final tops out at ~0.009% (the worst relative drift in the
profile); everything else is 0.003% or tighter.
Mid-generation coverage:
dec.logits_raw.gen20  The logits produced by the decoder's 20th
autoregressive step — i.e. pass the KV-
cached decoder 20 tokens beyond the prompt
prefix and capture the pre-argmax logits
that would predict the 21st generated
token. This is the only tensor-level test
of the n_past>0 KV cache read/write path;
the dec.* tensors above only cover the
prompt pass (n_past=0). Drift (~5.9e-5 max,
~2.1e-5 mean on the worst case) reflects
compounded fp32 error across 20 auto-
regressive steps + 4 transformer blocks +
cross-attention. C++ and ref both run their
own greedy loops matching the run() driver's
suppress_tokens / begin-suppress policy, so
argmax paths stay aligned through step 20.
